terms.fix: Design for partition tolerance always. Choose C vs A based on your use case.

star.s: Interview: design a payment system.
star.t: Choose DB considering CAP.
star.a: CP system (strong consistency) — cannot lose a transaction or show wrong balance. Accept reduced availability over wrong data.
star.r: Demonstrates understanding of trade-offs.

terms.what: Distributed lock: SET key value NX PX timeout — atomic set-if-not-exists with expiry. Cache stampede: all cache entries expire simultaneously — all requests hit DB at once.
terms.why: Distributed lock for leader election, resource exclusion across services. Cache stampede protection for high-traffic systems.
terms.how: Lock: SET lock:resource uuid NX PX 30000. If null → locked by others. Release: if (get(lock)==uuid) del(lock). Stampede fix: random TTL jitter (TTL + random(0,300s)).
terms.where: Distributed lock: payment deduplication, job scheduling, critical section across services. Stampede: product pages, homepage, any popular cached data.
terms.when: Use distributed lock for cross-service mutual exclusion. Use jitter to prevent stampede on regularly refreshed caches.
terms.issue: Lock expiry before operation completes — another process gets lock while first still holding it.
terms.fix: Extend lock TTL if operation is running longer than expected. Use Redisson library — handles lock extension automatically.

star.s: Cache for product page set to expire every midnight — all 1M users hit DB simultaneously at midnight.
star.t: Fix.
star.a: Added ±30 minute random jitter to TTL.
star.r: Expiries spread over 1-hour window. DB load normalized.

subsections.title: 🟢 TIER 3 — Technical Breadth (Study Over Next 2 Weeks)
subsections.keyRule: These 10 topics complete your technical profile for deep technical rounds when interviewers probe JVM internals, Kafka, Spring AOP/Transactions, and modern Java features. Study across week 2.

terms.what: Class Loader finds and loads .class files into JVM memory. Three built-in loaders: Bootstrap (rt.jar/JDK), Extension (ext/ directory), Application (your classpath).
terms.why: JVM does not load all classes at startup — classes are loaded on first use (lazy loading). Class Loaders make this possible.
terms.how: Parent delegation: when Application ClassLoader asked for a class, it asks Parent (Extension), which asks Parent (Bootstrap) first. If Bootstrap cannot find it, it comes back down. Prevents you from overriding java.lang.String.
terms.where: Every class load in JVM uses this chain. Custom ClassLoaders are used by: application servers (Tomcat), OSGi, hot-reload tools (JRebel).
terms.when: Custom ClassLoader needed when: loading classes from non-standard location, hot reloading, or class isolation in containers.
terms.issue: Same class loaded by two different ClassLoaders = two distinct classes in JVM — instanceof returns false even if same name. Causes ClassCastException.
terms.fix: Ensure only one ClassLoader loads a class. Common in application servers — check ClassLoader hierarchy in web.xml or container config.

star.s: ClassCastException on 'instanceof' check that seems correct.
star.t: Diagnose.
star.a: Same class loaded by parent ClassLoader and webapp ClassLoader — two classes. Fixed ClassLoader config in Tomcat.
star.r: One class, no exception.

terms.what: ZGC (Z Garbage Collector) is a low-latency GC with pause times under 1 millisecond regardless of heap size — even terabyte heaps.
terms.why: Some apps need consistent sub-millisecond latency — trading systems, real-time games, latency-sensitive microservices. G1GC cannot guarantee this.
terms.how: ZGC does almost all work concurrently with application threads. Uses colored pointers and load barriers. Pause time is nearly constant regardless of heap size.
terms.where: High-frequency trading, real-time systems, large-heap apps needing predictable latency.
terms.when: Use ZGC for: latency SLA under 10ms, heaps over 32GB, any latency-critical app. Note: slightly lower throughput than Parallel GC.
terms.issue: ZGC requires more CPU and memory overhead than G1GC — running concurrent GC threads has cost.
terms.fix: ZGC on Java 15+ is production-ready. Enable with -XX:+UseZGC. Monitor CPU overhead and ensure your environment has enough CPUs for concurrent GC.

star.s: Financial app has GC pause spikes of 500ms — unacceptable for trading.
star.t: Eliminate pauses.
star.a: Switched to ZGC (-XX:+UseZGC).
star.r: Max pause < 2ms. Trading SLA met.

terms.what: A Topic is a named stream of events — like a DB table but for events. Each Topic is split into Partitions — ordered, immutable logs. Partitions enable parallel processing.
terms.why: Without partitions, one consumer reads all events — cannot scale. With 10 partitions and 10 consumers, 10x parallel processing.
terms.how: Topic 'orders' with 10 partitions. Messages with same key (customerId) always go to same partition — preserving order per customer. Each partition has a sequential offset.
terms.where: Decoupled event-driven microservices: order-service publishes 'order-placed', multiple services consume independently.
terms.when: More partitions = more parallelism but more overhead. Rule: number of consumers <= number of partitions. Add partitions only when needed.
terms.issue: Increasing partition count for an existing topic reassigns messages — breaks ordering guarantees for key-based routing.
terms.fix: Plan partition count at topic creation. Start with enough (10-50 for high-volume). Cannot reduce partitions — only increase.

star.s: Interview: how does Kafka scale consumers?
star.t: Answer.
star.a: Each consumer in a group gets exclusive partitions. Add consumers up to partition count = linear scaling. Beyond partition count, extra consumers idle.
star.r: Correct explanation of consumer group scaling.

terms.what: Consumer reads events from Kafka topics. Consumers in the same Consumer Group share partitions — each partition assigned to one consumer. Different groups each get all events independently.
terms.why: Consumer groups enable parallel processing (one group) AND fan-out (multiple groups each process all events — order-service AND inventory-service both get all order events).
terms.how: @KafkaListener(topics='orders', groupId='inventory-service') void processOrder(OrderEvent event) { ... } — inventory-service processes all orders.
terms.where: Every service that reacts to events from another service.
terms.when: One consumer group per downstream service. Each service independently reads at its own pace — Kafka retains messages for both.

terms.what: IoC Container is Spring's object factory. It creates beans, wires dependencies, and manages bean lifecycle. ApplicationContext is the main IoC container interface.
terms.why: Without IoC, you manually create objects and pass dependencies — tight coupling, hard to test. IoC delegates creation to Spring — loose coupling, easy testing.
terms.how: @SpringBootApplication starts up ApplicationContext. Spring scans @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller — creates beans. You just @Autowired where needed.
terms.where: The foundation of every Spring application. All beans live here.
terms.when: Always let Spring manage your beans. Only create objects with 'new' for: simple value objects (DTO, entity), objects that should NOT be Spring-managed.
terms.issue: Trying to use @Autowired in a class created with 'new' — null injection. Spring cannot inject into manually-created objects.
terms.fix: Let Spring create and inject every bean. If you need a prototype bean, inject ApplicationContext and call getBean().

star.s: @Autowired repository is null in service.
star.t: Diagnose.
star.a: Service was created with 'new Service()' in controller — not managed by Spring. Changed to @Autowired injection.
star.r: Dependencies injected correctly.

terms.term: 🟢 T3 #6 — AOP Terms: Aspect, Advice, Pointcut, JoinPoint
terms.what: Aspect = class with cross-cutting logic (@Aspect). Advice = when to run (@Before, @After, @Around, @AfterReturning, @AfterThrowing). Pointcut = which methods to intercept. JoinPoint = the actual method being intercepted.
terms.why: Understanding AOP terminology helps you write correct aspects and debug proxy- related issues.

terms.what: Propagation defines what happens when a @Transactional method is called from another @Transactional context. 7 types: REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, NESTED, SUPPORTS, MANDATORY, NEVER, NOT_SUPPORTED.
terms.why: Correct propagation prevents: accidentally nesting transactions, losing rollbacks, or creating transactions where they don't belong.
terms.how: REQUIRED (default): join existing or create new. REQUIRES_NEW: always create new, suspend outer. NESTED: create savepoint within current (rolls back to savepoint on error). SUPPORTS: use existing if present, else no transaction.
terms.where: Any @Transactional method. Critical for: audit logging (should save even if main tx rolls back → REQUIRES_NEW), batch processing, and nested service calls.
terms.when: Use REQUIRED for most cases. REQUIRES_NEW for operations that must commit independently (audit, notification). NESTED for partial rollback within a transaction.
terms.issue: REQUIRED propagation: inner method rollback = outer transaction also rolled back — all work lost.
terms.fix: For operations that must succeed even if outer tx fails: use REQUIRES_NEW. This creates an independent transaction.

star.s: Audit log not saved when order transaction fails.
star.t: Fix.
star.a: @Transactional(propagation=REQUIRES_NEW) on audit method.
star.r: Audit saves in its own transaction regardless of outer tx outcome.

terms.what: Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM — not OS threads. You can create millions of them with tiny memory footprint.
terms.why: Platform threads (OS threads) are expensive — each needs 1MB+ stack. Can't have more than ~10,000. Virtual threads need ~few KB each — millions possible.
terms.how: Thread.ofVirtual().start(() -> handleRequest()). Or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() — creates a new virtual thread per task.
terms.where: High-concurrency servers, each request gets its own virtual thread. I/O-bound workloads where threads spend most time waiting.
terms.when: Use virtual threads for I/O-bound concurrent work (HTTP calls, DB queries, file reads). NOT for CPU-bound tasks — no benefit there.
terms.issue: Synchronized blocks pin virtual threads to platform threads — loses the benefit.
terms.fix: Replace synchronized with ReentrantLock in code that will use virtual threads. Avoid long-running synchronized blocks.

star.s: Tomcat with 200 platform threads cannot handle 10,000 concurrent connections.
star.t: Scale.
star.a: Switched to virtual thread executor. Each connection gets its own virtual thread.
star.r: Handles 100,000 concurrent connections on same machine.

terms.term: 🟢 T3 #9 — Upskilling Strategy for 12+ Year Engineers
terms.what: Structured approach to staying technically sharp while growing leadership influence. Focus on three layers: depth (go deeper in your core stack), breadth (understand adjacent technologies), and visibility (document and share what you know).
terms.why: Technology changes fast. A 12-year engineer who stopped learning at year 8 is effectively a 4-year engineer with 12 years of salary expectation. Continuous learning is non-negotiable for staying relevant and promotable.
terms.how: Weekly habits: 1) Read one architecture blog per week (Martin Fowler, AWS Architecture Blog, Google Cloud Blog, InfoQ). 2) One technical book per quarter (Designing Data-Intensive Applications, Building Microservices, Clean Architecture). 3) 30 minutes daily on new tech (hands-on, not just reading). Monthly: contribute one ADR (Architecture Decision Record) for a team decision. Quarterly: deliver one tech talk or brown bag session. Yearly: get one certification (AWS/GCP Solutions Architect, CKA for Kubernetes, Spring Professional). Specific areas to focus for 2025: Observability (distributed tracing, OpenTelemetry), AI/LLM integration in healthcare (FHIR + AI for condition detection), cloud cost optimization (FinOps), and data streaming (Kafka → Apache Flink).
terms.where: Personal learning is most effective in small daily habits. Apply new learning immediately on real work — not toy projects.
terms.when: Consistent > intense. 30 minutes daily is better than 8-hour weekend sprints. Schedule it like a meeting — it disappears without scheduling.
terms.issue: Tutorial hell: watching/reading about technologies without hands-on practice. The illusion of learning without actual skill development.
terms.fix: Build something with every new technology you learn. For rate limiting: implement a Redis rate limiter from scratch. For Kubernetes: deploy your own service on GKE with HPA and liveness probes. The act of building surfaces all the gaps that tutorials miss.

star.s: Job market became more competitive. Java 8 skills alone no longer sufficient for senior positions. Colleagues with cloud and architecture skills getting promoted.
star.t: Modernize skill set while maintaining delivery on current project.
star.a: Created a personal learning roadmap: Q1: GKE + Kubernetes CKA certification. Q2: Reactive programming (Spring WebFlux, Project Reactor). Q3: OpenTelemetry + distributed tracing in eCR-Now. Q4: Kafka Streams for real-time condition monitoring. Applied each skill to real eCR-Now features within 2 weeks of learning.
star.r: Migrated eCR-Now to reactive WebClient (20% throughput improvement). Added distributed tracing (reduced incident investigation from 2 hours to 15 minutes). Passed CKA. Promoted to senior architect role.

terms.term: 🟢 T3 #10 — Excelling in Architecture Meetings & Tech Discussions
terms.what: Strategies for contributing effectively in architecture reviews, design sessions, and technical leadership meetings — where impact comes from clear thinking and communication, not just deep technical knowledge.
terms.why: At 12+ years, your influence is through others. How you communicate in architecture meetings determines whether your ideas get adopted, whether you are seen as a leader, and whether you get invited to higher-level discussions.
terms.how: Before the meeting: read any design docs or tickets. Identify the core tradeoffs. Prepare 1-2 clarifying questions and 1-2 concrete suggestions. In the meeting: 1) Listen first, understand the problem fully before proposing solutions. 2) When you speak: state assumption, then reasoning, then recommendation. 'My assumption is we need <99% availability. Given that, I'd recommend X over Y because...' 3) Quantify when possible: 'X adds 2ms latency per request — at 1000 req/sec that's 2s of cumulative overhead, is that acceptable?' 4) Acknowledge tradeoffs: 'X is simpler but Y is more scalable — what's our 3-year scale projection?' 5) When you disagree: 'I see the appeal of X. My concern is Y. Can we discuss how to mitigate that?' After the meeting: write the decision as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record). This positions you as the person who drives clarity and documentation.
terms.where: Architecture reviews, design sessions, incident post-mortems, technology evaluations, vendor discussions.
terms.when: Every time you are in a room with architects, staff engineers, or technical leadership. These are your opportunities to demonstrate seniority beyond your individual code contributions.
terms.issue: Experienced engineers often speak too technically too soon — alienating non-technical stakeholders and losing the room before making their point.
terms.fix: Match your communication depth to the audience. With engineers: full technical depth. With product managers: business impact and risk. With executives: cost, time, and risk in one sentence. Prepare audience-appropriate versions of your technical points.

star.s: Architecture review: debate about whether eCR-Now should use synchronous FHIR queries vs async event-driven. Two senior engineers on opposite sides. Meeting at risk of going in circles.
star.t: Facilitate to a clear recommendation and decision.
star.a: Drew a decision matrix on the whiteboard: criteria (latency, reliability, CDC compliance, implementation complexity) rated 1-5 for each option. Added quantitative estimates where available: sync adds 200ms latency but simplifies error handling. Async reduces latency but adds Kafka operational complexity. Named the key constraint: CDC 24-hour window gives us slack — eventual consistency is acceptable. Recommended: async for primary flow, sync as fallback for urgent cases. Proposed to write ADR to document decision and revisit in 6 months.
star.r: Meeting ended with clear decision. ADR written and approved. Both engineers aligned. Decision referenced 6 months later when adding a new EHR integration — saved 2 weeks of re-debate.

subsections.title: ⚡ Quick Reference — Last 24 Hours Before Interview
subsections.keyRule: High-yield communication tips, project walkthrough summary, and concurrency traps for the night before or morning of your interview.

terms.term: ⚡ QR #1 — Technical Communication for Senior Roles
terms.what: Senior engineers communicate differently: they explain WHY before HOW, quantify impact, acknowledge tradeoffs, mention what they considered and rejected, and connect technical decisions to business outcomes.
terms.why: At architect/staff level, you influence decisions across teams. Your ability to communicate risk, tradeoffs, and recommendations clearly determines your actual impact — often more than your coding ability.
terms.how: Key habits: 1) Always state the 'why' before the 'how'. 2) Quantify: 'reduced latency from 3s to 180ms' beats 'improved performance'. 3) Acknowledge tradeoffs: 'We chose X over Y. X gives us A but costs B. We accepted that because...' 4) Mention rejected alternatives: 'We considered polling but chose event-driven because the 5-minute polling lag was too long for CDC 24-hour window.' 5) Connect to business: 'This change reduced CDC report failures, keeping us in compliance and avoiding the $X per-incident penalty.' 6) Invite follow-up: End explanations with 'Any questions on that before I move on?' — shows confidence and controls pace.
terms.where: All stakeholder communication, architecture reviews, incident post-mortems, design documents, 1:1s with management.
terms.when: Always. This mindset shift from 'I built X' to 'I designed X to solve Y because Z, which resulted in W' is what separates senior from staff engineer levels.
terms.issue: Technical people often go too deep too fast — losing non-technical stakeholders in implementation details before establishing why it matters.
terms.fix: Practice the Pyramid Principle: Start with the conclusion/recommendation, then give supporting reasons, then details. Adjust depth based on audience. Read body language — if eyes glaze, zoom out. If they lean forward, go deeper.

star.s: CTO asked why eCR-Now needs to be on GKE vs a simple EC2 instance.
star.t: Justify architectural decision concisely to a non-GKE-expert CTO.
star.a: Said: 'Hospital admission volume spikes 10x during morning rounds. A single EC2 needs to be sized for peak at all times — 90% idle cost. GKE auto-scales pods in 30 seconds based on CPU/memory, so we only pay for what we use. Also, GKE gives us automatic restarts when a pod freezes due to a stuck FHIR connection — critical for our 24-hour CDC reporting SLA.' Three sentences, business-framed.
star.r: CTO immediately approved GKE budget. Technical decision accepted because it was communicated in cost and SLA terms, not Kubernetes terms.

terms.term: ⚡ QR #2 — Scenario: Explain Your Project to a New Team / Architecture Panel
terms.what: A structured approach to explaining the eCR-Now / EHR integration system to technical and semi-technical audiences at architecture level. Covers business context, component responsibilities, data flow, technology choices, failure handling, and observability.
terms.why: Architects must explain systems clearly to: new team members, senior leadership, external partners (new EHR vendors), auditors (for HIPAA compliance), and interviewers. Each audience needs a different depth but the same structure.
terms.how: For technical audience (architecture panel): Start with a diagram (whiteboard or slides). Draw from left to right — IMS → Linus → GKE (eCR-Now) → FHIR Server → CDC → EHR. Then narrate the happy path: 'Patient admitted in IMS. IMS uses cpXML to resolve FHIR IDs, sends launchPatient POST to Linus Gateway. Linus authenticates (client cert), rate limits, routes to one of N eCR-Now pods on GKE.' Then the FHIR step: 'eCR-Now queries the EHR FHIR server for Condition, Observation, MedicationRequest resources for this patient-encounter. Evaluates each code against RCTC trigger code set. If match found: reportable.' Then CDC step: 'Generates eCR CDA document. Sends via Direct messaging (SMTP S/MIME through our HISP to CDC's HISP). Polls our Direct IMAP inbox every 15 minutes for CDC response.' Then EHR update: 'When CDC response received, eCR-Now POSTs a FHIR DocumentReference to Evident EHR. EHR displays using HL7 stylesheet.' Then failure handling: 'Circuit breaker on FHIR calls. Kafka retry queue for CDC submissions. Liveness probe restarts stuck pods. Dead-letter queue for permanently failed reports with PagerDuty alert.' Then observability: 'Prometheus metrics per step. Grafana dashboard. Distributed tracing with correlation ID per patient-encounter.'
terms.where: Architecture reviews, new hire onboarding, partner integration meetings, auditor walkthroughs, conference presentations.
terms.when: Prepare 3 versions: 5 minutes (high-level), 15 minutes (technical depth), 45 minutes (full design review with Q&A). Have the diagram ready — always draw it, never describe it purely verbally for complex systems.
terms.issue: Going too deep too fast on one component (e.g., spending 10 minutes on the IMAP polling logic) while the audience does not yet have the full system picture. They cannot evaluate depth without the breadth context first.
terms.fix: Always do one full pass at 10,000 feet before going deep on any component. After the overview: 'Any questions before I go deeper on any specific part?' This hands control to the audience and ensures you spend depth time on what they actually care about.

star.s: New EHR vendor (not Evident) evaluating whether their FHIR server can integrate with eCR-Now. Technical and product stakeholders in the room.
star.t: Explain eCR-Now integration requirements clearly so they can assess their FHIR server's compatibility.
star.a: Drew the integration diagram. Focused on the EHR-facing side: launchPatient payload requirements (FHIR IDs, fhirUrl format), FHIR queries eCR-Now would send (resources, parameters, expected response format), DocumentReference POST format for CDC response. Gave them a written FHIR API contract document. Answered their SMART on FHIR auth questions (eCR-Now supports both client credentials and backend services OAuth2 FHIR auth flows).
star.r: EHR vendor confirmed compatibility in 2 hours. Integration started 2 weeks later. eCR-Now now integrated with 3 EHR vendors using the same architecture.

terms.term: ⚡ QR #3 — STAR: Migrating eCR-Now to GKE for Autoscaling
terms.what: STAR story demonstrating cloud architecture decision-making, migration planning, and operational improvement through containerization and Kubernetes orchestration.
terms.why: Shows ability to evaluate infrastructure options, make and justify architectural decisions, manage migration risk in a healthcare compliance system, and deliver quantifiable operational improvements.
terms.how: Situation: eCR-Now running on two fixed EC2 instances. During morning rounds (7-9am), admission volume spiked 10x. EC2 sized for 2x normal load — 5x admissions during peak caused a backlog of 200+ reports, some taking 45 minutes to process. Non-peak hours: 90% CPU idle — paying for unused capacity. Task: Design infrastructure that handles 10x burst without degrading report generation time, while reducing cost at off-peak. Action: 1) Evaluated options: vertical scaling (bigger EC2 — expensive, still fixed), horizontal with manual scaling (operational burden), Kubernetes autoscaling (complex setup, best long-term). Recommended GKE for: autoscaling, self-healing (pod restart on stuck), rolling deployments, multi-zone HA. 2) Containerized Spring Boot app: multi-stage Dockerfile, optimized JVM flags for containers (-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75 instead of fixed -Xmx). 3) Kubernetes manifests: Deployment (min 2 pods, max 20), HPA (CPU 70%), readiness probe, liveness probe, PDB (min 2 available). 4) Zero-downtime migration: ran GKE and EC2 in parallel for 2 weeks. Health checks validated. Traffic cut over to GKE. EC2 decommissioned after 1 week of stable GKE. 5) Post-migration: switched to KEDA on Kafka lag for faster scale-up than CPU HPA. Result: Morning burst backlog eliminated (max 2 minutes report latency at peak). Infrastructure cost reduced 35% (EC2 was always-on, GKE scales to 2 pods at night). Zero downtime during migration.
terms.where: Infrastructure architecture decision for eCR-Now. GCP project, GKE cluster, same region as hospital networks.
terms.when: Migration took 6 weeks end-to-end: 2 weeks containerization + testing, 2 weeks parallel run, 2 weeks validation and EC2 decommission.
terms.issue: Spring Boot JVM startup time in containers was 25 seconds. During scale-up events, new pods took 25 seconds before serving traffic. Readiness probe prevented premature routing, but requests queued for 25 seconds on scale-up.
terms.fix: Predictive pre-scaling with CronJob (scale to 10 pods at 6:30am before peak). KEDA starts scaling earlier (based on queue depth, not CPU lag). Future: Spring Boot AOT + CRaC for sub-second startup.

star.s: eCR-Now on fixed EC2: 45-minute delays during morning peaks, 90% idle at night. Cost: paying for peak capacity 24/7.
star.t: Handle 10x admission burst without delay. Reduce infrastructure cost.
star.a: Designed and led migration to GKE: containerization, Kubernetes manifests, KEDA autoscaling on Kafka lag, CronJob pre-scaling, zero-downtime migration plan. Led 2-engineer team for 6 weeks.
star.r: Morning peak delay: 45 min → under 2 min. Infrastructure cost: 35% reduction. Zero downtime during migration. Self-healing: 3 pod restarts in first month (stuck threads) fully automatic.

terms.what: Deadlock happens when two or more threads are each waiting for a lock held by the other — they wait forever. No thread can make progress. App freezes.
terms.why: Thread A holds Lock1 and needs Lock2. Thread B holds Lock2 and needs Lock1. Both wait forever. JVM has no automatic deadlock resolution.
terms.how: Classic: synchronized(lock1) { synchronized(lock2) {...} } in one thread, reversed order in another. If both threads run simultaneously, deadlock.
terms.where: Complex locking scenarios with multiple resources. Payment processing (lock account A, lock account B), database deadlocks.
terms.when: Deadlocks happen under concurrent load that you cannot always reproduce in tests. Requires careful lock ordering discipline.
terms.issue: Deadlocks can be rare and hard to reproduce — might only happen under specific timing conditions.
terms.fix: 1) Always acquire locks in the same order in all threads. 2) Use tryLock() with timeout. 3) Use higher-level abstractions (Database transactions, java.util.concurrent).

star.s: Transfer service deadlocks when two users transfer to each other simultaneously.
star.t: Fix.
star.a: Normalize lock order — always lock account with smaller ID first.
star.r: No more deadlock regardless of transfer direction.
